title: Charmed Life - Chapter 7 (Fragment 3)
text:
Gwendolen got up and went without a word. As Julia and Millie did not come back, the dining table seemed rather empty that evening. It was all stocks and shares from Bernard at one end, and statues again from Mr. Saunders at the other.

Cat found that Gwendolen was rather triumphant. She felt she had made an impression on Chrestomanci at last. So she returned to the attack with a will on Sunday.

On Sunday the Family dressed in its best and walked down to Morning Service at the village church. Witches are not supposed to like church. Nor are they supposed to be able to work magic there. But this never bothered Gwendolen at all. Mrs. Sharp had many times remarked on it, as showing what exceptional talents Gwendolen had. Gwendolen sat next to Cat in the Chrestomanci pew, looking the picture of demure innocence in her broiderie anglaise Sunday dress and hat, and found her place in her prayer book as if she were truly saintly.

The village people nudged one another and whispered about her. This rather pleased Gwendolen. She liked to be well known. She kept up the pretense of saintliness until the sermon had begun.

The vicar climbed shakily into the pulpit and gave his text in a weak, wandering voice. “For there were many in the congregation that were not sanctified.” This was certainly to the point. Unfortunately, nothing else he said was. He told, in his weak, wandering voice, of weak, wandering episodes in his early life. He compared them with weak, wandering things he thought were happening in the world today. He told them they had better be sanctified or all sorts of things—which he forgot to mention—would happen, which reminded him of a weak, wandering thing his aunts used to tell him.

Mr. Saunders was asleep by this time, and so was stocks-and-shares Bernard. The old lady with mittens was nodding. One of the saints in the stained-glass windows yawned, and put up his crosier elegantly to cover his mouth. He looked around at his neighbor, who was a formidable nun. Her robes hung in severe folds, like a bundle of sticks. The bishop stretched out his stained-glass crosier and tapped the nun on the shoulder. She resented it. She marched into his window and began shaking him.

Cat saw her. He saw the colored, transparent bishop clouting the nun over the wimple, and the nun giving him as good as she got. Meanwhile, the hairy saint next to them made a dive for his neighbor, who was a kingly sort of saint, holding a model of the Castle. The kingly saint dropped his model and fled for protection, in a twinkle of glassy feet, behind the robes of a simpering lady saint. The hairy saint jumped gleefully up and down on the model of the Castle.

One by one, all the windows came to life. Almost every saint turned and fought the one next to him. Those who had no one to fight either hitched up their robes and did silly dances, or waved to the vicar, who rambled on without noticing. The little tiny people blowing trumpets in the corners of the windows sprang and gamboled and frisked, and pulled transparent faces at anyone who was looking. The hairy saint winkled the kingly one out from behind the simpering lady and chased him from window to window in and out of all the other fighting couples.

vocabulary.definition: To begin an aggressive action or effort again.
vocabulary.example: She returned to the attack with a will on Sunday.

vocabulary.definition: A type of white open embroidery with eyelets.
vocabulary.example: She wore a broderie anglaise Sunday dress and hat.

vocabulary.definition: To become active or animated.
vocabulary.example: One by one, all the windows came to life.

vocabulary.definition: Hitting or striking hard.
vocabulary.example: The bishop was clouting the nun over the wimple.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people assembled for religious worship.
vocabulary.example: For there were many in the congregation that were not sanctified.

vocabulary.definition: A hooked staff carried by bishops as a symbol of office.
vocabulary.example: One of the saints put up his crosier elegantly to cover his mouth.

vocabulary.definition: Reserved, modest, and shy (usually describes a woman).
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen sat next to Cat in the Chrestomanci pew, looking the picture of demure innocence.

vocabulary.definition: Ran away from danger or pursuer.
vocabulary.example: The kingly saint dropped his model and fled for protection.

vocabulary.definition: Inspiring fear or respect through being large, powerful, or intense.
vocabulary.example: Her neighbor was a formidable nun.

vocabulary.definition: Moved about playfully.
vocabulary.example: The little tiny people frisked and pulled transparent faces.

vocabulary.definition: Ran or jumped about playfully.
vocabulary.example: The little tiny people sprang and gamboled in the corners of the windows.

vocabulary.definition: In a very happy or joyful way, often with a sense of mischievousness.
vocabulary.example: The hairy saint jumped gleefully up and down on the model of the Castle.

vocabulary.definition: To lift or raise something, especially clothing.
vocabulary.example: Those who had no one to fight either hitched up their robes and danced.

vocabulary.definition: An effect produced on the mind or feelings.
vocabulary.example: She felt she had made an impression on Chrestomanci at last.

vocabulary.definition: Walked in a determined or structured manner.
vocabulary.example: She marched into his window and began shaking him.

vocabulary.definition: To poke someone gently, usually to attract attention.
vocabulary.example: The village people nudged one another and whispered about her.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling happy or satisfied.
vocabulary.example: This rather pleased Gwendolen.

vocabulary.definition: An attempt to make something that is not the case appear true.
vocabulary.example: She kept up the pretense of saintliness until the sermon had begun.

vocabulary.definition: The act of keeping someone or something safe from harm.
vocabulary.example: He fled for protection behind the robes of a lady saint.

vocabulary.definition: A raised platform in a church for the preacher to speak from.
vocabulary.example: The vicar climbed shakily into the pulpit.

vocabulary.definition: To feel bitterness or indignation at a circumstance or action.
vocabulary.example: She resented it when the bishop tapped her on the shoulder.

vocabulary.definition: Long flowing garments worn by clergy or monks.
vocabulary.example: Her robes hung in severe folds.

vocabulary.definition: The quality of being saintly, holy or virtuous.
vocabulary.example: She kept up the pretense of saintliness until the sermon had begun.

vocabulary.definition: Made holy or purified.
vocabulary.example: There were many in the congregation that were not sanctified.

vocabulary.definition: A talk on a religious or moral subject at a church service.
vocabulary.example: She kept up the pretense of saintliness until the sermon had begun.

vocabulary.definition: Very great; intense; strict or harsh in manner.
vocabulary.example: Her robes hung in severe folds.

vocabulary.definition: Pretentiously or affectedly coy or ingratiating.
vocabulary.example: He fled behind the robes of a simpering lady saint.

vocabulary.definition: Having achieved a victory or success; exulting.
vocabulary.example: Cat found that Gwendolen was rather triumphant.

vocabulary.definition: A priest in charge of a parish in the Church of England.
vocabulary.example: The vicar climbed shakily into the pulpit and gave his text.

vocabulary.definition: A cloth worn around the head and neck by some nuns.
vocabulary.example: The bishop was clouting the nun over the wimple.
